title: Conducting Polymer Hydrogels as 3D Electrodes: Applications for Supercapacitors
abstract:
Communications
1214
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1999
0935-9648/99/1410-1214 $ 17.50+.50/0
Adv. Mater. 1999, 11, No. 14
cules 1b-4 and 1b-5 as the prototype of optimized nonlin-
earity-stability-solubility characteristics, whereby a typical
mb value of 22 000  10±48 esu is found for molecule 1b-5).
This opens an interesting route to poled materials for elec-
trooptic-modulation and photorefractive applications.[32,33]
The results also demonstrate that the cubic polarizabilities
increase much more with conjugated length compared to
quadratic polarizabilities, leading to enhanced cubic non-
linearities (up to g(0) values of about 7400 ´ 10±36 esu). In
addition, the present work emphasizes the challenging
potential of new engineering routes towards the optimiza-
tion of materials with large cubic nonlinearities via increas-
ing the magnitude of the g peaks.
Experimental
Polyenals of the series 1a and 2a were obtained from the corresponding
generic aldehydes via a sequential protocol based upon Wittig oxyprenyla-
tion followed by acidic hydrolysis [9]. The synthesis of push-pull derivatives
1±2,b±c was carried out by reacting 1a-n (or 2a-n) with one equivalent of
malonodinitrile (or methylrhodanine) in refluxing ethanol. After evapora-
tion of the solvent, the crude compounds were purified by column chroma-
tography and recrystallization. Pure all-trans compounds were obtained as
assessed by NMR, elemental analyses, and mass spectrometry. The DSC re-
cords were recorded in air using a Perkin-Elmer DSC 7 microcalorimeter.
CV was performed using a classical three electrodes cell as described in the
literature [9].
Dipole moments were obtained from dielectric and refractive index mea-
surement performed in dry toluene as carried out in the literature [9], except
in the case for molecules of the series 2b,c, for which m values derived from
electrooptical measurements were used [20]. EFISH measurements were
carried out using the experimental protocol and quartz reference described
in the literature [9]. The experimental accuracy ranges between 5 and 10 %.
For each molecule THG experiments were conducted using solutions of five
different concentrations in chloroform and the experimental protocol de-
scribed in the literature [34]. The measurements were calibrated relative to
fused silica for which a w3 value of 2.8´10±14 esu (3.9´10±22 m2V±2) was used
[35]. The experimental accuracy amounts to 5 %.

Final version: June 16, 1999

Conducting Polymer Hydrogels as 3D
Electrodes: Applications for Supercapacitors**
By Soumyadeb Ghosh* and Olle Inganäs
Electrochemical systems essentially involve processes at
the interface between an electrode and an electrolyte solu-
tion. The larger the area of the interface, the larger will be
the rate of the process. This has been a motivating factor
for fabrication of porous electrodesÐhigh surface-area
electrodes with structure elements down to nanometer
scale have been reported.[1,2] Conducting polymers have
been used as electrode material for various applications.[3,4]
These polymers are generally permeable to small mole-
±
[*] Dr. S. Ghosh, Dr. O. Inganäs
Department of Physics and Measurement Technology
Linköping University
S-58183 Linköping (Sweden)
[**] Funding from the TFR (Sweden), is gratefully acknowledged. The
PEDOT-PSS sample (Baytron-P) was a gift from Bayer AG, Germany.
 cules.[4] This, in principle, should allow every volume ele-
ment of the electrode material at the molecular level to be
in contact with the electrolyte solution, resulting in an ex-
tremely high effective surface area. However, due to their
low compatibility with the electrolyte phase, the separa-
tions of the polymer chains in conducting polymers are
generally small relative to the double layer thickness. This
leads to high electrolyte resistance in the polymer matrix[4]
and low effective double layer surface area. Compatibility
of conducting polymers has been improved in recent years,
and dispersions/solutions of conducting polymers are now
widely available.[3] Employment of such conducting disper-
sions indeed improves the kinetics of the electrochemical
process, which is then limited by the diffusion of ions in the
colloidal particles of the polymer.[5]
We have shown recently[6] that an aqueous dispersion of
the
conducting
polymer
poly(3,4-ethylene-dioxythio-
phene)-poly(styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT-PSS, trade name
Baytron-P, commercially available from Bayer AG, Ger-
many), is a dispersion of a non-stoichiometric polyelectro-
lyte complex of PEDOT and PSS, with an excess of the lat-
ter component. Thus the colloidal particles are negatively
chargedÐwhich stabilizes them in the aqueous mediaÐ
and are in a highly swelled state. Further, when distributed
in another polymer matrix as a blend, the negatively
charged particles can be ionically crosslinked by multiva-
lent cations into a solid with a network morphology.[7]
In this paper we present the preparation of novel, highly
swelled conducting-polymer hydrogel electrodes. The high
porosity/swellability of the material is achieved by an inge-
nious but simple preparation method where the aqueous
dispersion of PEDOT-PSS is ionically crosslinked into a
nanometer-scale conducting network.
A direct application of porous electrodes is found in
supercapacitors,[8±11] which are energy storage devices, simi-
lar to batteries, but with much higher power density than
typical batteries.[2,8,9] The growing interest in these devices,
particularly in industrial research laboratories, is stimulated
by their potential application in high power equipment and
machines such as electric vehicles,[12] high-energy pulsed la-
sers,[9] camcorders, cellular phones, etc.[13] Conducting poly-
mers have also been used as supercapacitor electrodes[10,11]
in which energy is stored by a reversible electrochemical re-
action known as doping/undoping.[3] However, low ionic
mobility in the polymer matrix lowers the maximum power
density of such electrodes. In the present work, this defi-
ciency of conducting polymers has been addressed, and the
applicability of the conducting hydrogels to high power-
density supercapacitor electrodes is demonstrated.
For the preparation of the PEDOT-PSS electrode, a film
of the polymer was cast onto a gold electrode from its dis-
persion (Baytron-P) and then equilibrated with a 0.25 M
MgSO4 solution for the ionic crosslinking. Cyclic voltam-
mograms of a two-electrode cell of such electrodes in sym-
metrical supercapacitor geometry[11] in an aqueous electro-
lyte is shown in Figure 1. The voltammograms have
rectangular shape, characteristic for a capacitor, even at ex-
tremely high scan rates. This reveals the high rate of elec-
trochemical process that can be achieved in these elec-
trodes, as confirmed by the results from impedance
measurements and chrono-potentiometric studies of the
electrodes (discussed later). During the equilibration in the
aqueous medium for the crosslinking, the PEDOT-PSS film
swells, but ionic crosslinking of the excess anionic sulfonate
groups by the bivalent Mg2+ ions prevents the polymer
coating from disintegrating (as it does in pure water). This
results in a swelled hydrogel (water content of about 90 %
in 0.25 M MgSO4) with high ionic mobility, and having
electronic conductivity in the network through the conduct-
ing polymer chains of PEDOT. This material, nonetheless,
has some disadvantages with regard to its application in
supercapacitors: The maximum energy density of the mate-
rial is not very large due to the high molecular mass of the
PEDOT-PSS units; too much water content increases the
weight of the supercapacitors; and the mechanical strength
of the material is poor. To improve on these properties, an-
other electroactive conducting polymer, polypyrrole (PPy),
was grown electrochemically on the PEDOT-PSS elec-
trode. The resulting polymer composite (designated as
PEDOT-PSS/PPy) also has a high water content (about
75 %) and has much better mechanical properties. It can be
peeled from the electrode as a free standing film. The cyclic
voltammograms of the supercapacitor containing the latter
material are also shown in Figure 1. They show that PED-
OT-PSS/PPy also has capacitor-like characteristics, even at
very high scan rates, though not at rates as high as those ob-
tained for the PEDOT-PSS electrodes. Nevertheless, the
PEDOT-PSS/PPy electrodes have much higher capacitance
than the PEDOT-PSS electrode. The water content in the
electrode can be controlled by only partially drying the dis-
persion before the crosslinking. When the dispersion was
dried only to about one third of its weight, a PEDOT-PSS/

scale have been reported.[1,2] Conducting polymers have
been used as electrode material for various applications.[3,4]

cules.[4] This, in principle, should allow every volume ele-

in contact with the electrolyte solution, resulting in an ex-

sions indeed improves the kinetics of the electrochemical

phene)-poly(styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT-PSS, trade name
Baytron-P, commercially available from Bayer AG, Ger-
many), is a dispersion of a non-stoichiometric polyelectro-

PEDOT-PSS/PPy electrodes have much higher capacitance
than the PEDOT-PSS electrode. The water content in the
electrode can be controlled by only partially drying the dis-
persion before the crosslinking. When the dispersion was
dried only to about one third of its weight, a PEDOT-PSS/

PPy composite with about 95 % water content could be ob-
tained (let us call this composite w-PEDOT-PSS/PPy). This
material has similar mechanical and electrochemical prop-
erties to that of PEDOT-PSS/PPy.
The morphology of a slowly dried w-PEDOT-PSS/PPy
electrode, imaged using atomic force microscopy (AFM) in
the tapping mode,[14] is shown in Figure 2. The micrograph
shows the rough morphology of the polymer surface even
in the dry state. However, no deep pores were observed,
which is consistent with its hydrogel-like structure. It is in-
teresting to observe that the material is an assembly of
spherical grains (Fig. 2B), evidence that the material was
prepared by crosslinking the PEDOT-PSS colloidal parti-
cles. In the swollen state of the material, the grains are ex-
pected to be swelled (as the particles were in the dispersed
state). It is important to note that no separate phase for the
PPy could be identified, which indicates that the latter is
homogeneously blended with the PEDOT-PSS phase on
electropolymerization. This was further confirmed by Au-
ger electron spectroscopy of the electrode surface, where
peaks due to both the sulfur atoms from the PEDOT-PSS
and the nitrogen atoms from the PPy could be observed.
Thus the interaction of the electrolyte solution with the
electrode material is not through the pores, but through the
bulk of the material as a homogenous phase.
The applicability of the electrodes in supercapacitors was
directly evaluated by means of chrono-potentiometric stud-
ies on the galvanostatic charging±discharging of the super-
capacitors at various current densities. It is well-known that
the energy output of a supercapacitor is dependent on the
power density at which the energy is extracted.[15] At high
power densities, the electrode process becomes slow com-
pared to the high rate of charge flow, which leads to an in-
crease in the internal resistance and hence to a loss of
energy. Typically, the best electrochemical supercapacitors
can have energy densities in the range of 10 Wh/kg of the
active electrode material, but only a fraction of this value is
obtained at power densities above 1 kW/kg.[10] Therefore,
it is most appropriate to present the energy and power den-
sitiesÐcalculated[11] from the chronopotentiometric studies
(details in the Experimental section) on the supercapaci-
tors with different electrode materialsÐin form of Ragone
plots[15] (Fig. 3). It can be observed that for both the PED-
OT-PSS and the PEDOT-PSS/PPy electrodes, extremely
high values of power densities can be obtained without
much decrease in the extractable energy of the supercapac-
itors. For the latter material, the energy density is much

electrode with scan size of 30 mm ´ 30 mm (A), and 1 mm ´ 1 mm (B). AFM
images were obtained with a Nanoscope IIIa instrument (Digital Instru-
ments, Santa Barbara, CA, USA) in the standard tapping mode.

PSS/PPy; e) PEDOT-PSS. See Experimental section for details.
higher than that in the former, agreeing with its higher ca-
pacitance value obtained from the cyclic voltammetry. This
is partly due to the lower molecular weight of PPy mono-
mer units compared to that of the PEDOT-PSS, which al-
lows the storage of a higher amount of charge per unit
weight of the material. Further, reinforcement of the con-
ducting network in the swelled polymer on electropolymer-
ization of PPy may also contribute to the higher energy
density in PEDOT-PSS/PPy supercapacitors. The Ragone
plots show a slightly better performance of the w-PEDOT-
PSS/PPy than PEDOT-PSS/PPy as electrode material. In
contrast to the hydrogel electrodes, the energy densities of
pure PPy-coated electrodes and the unswelled PEDOT-
PSS/PPy electrodes, where PPy was grown from acetoni-
trile medium on unswelled PEDOT-PSS, decrease sharply
on increasing the power density. For example, at low power
density (about 100 W/kg), the supercapacitor with pure
PPy electrodes has a similar energy density to that in the
PEDOT-PSS/PPy-containing devices. However, only one
fifth of that energy is obtained from the PPy electrodes as
the power density reaches a value of 3 kW/kg, whereas no
loss in energy density is observed for PEDOT-PSS/PPy
electrodes. From another view, it is observed that the
swelled electrodes can deliver the stored energy at power
densities more than 25 times higher than at which any
meaningful amount of energy could be obtained from the
unswelled electrodes. This highlights the importance of the
morphology of the material and the extent of the improve-
ment in the supercapacitor that can be brought about by
proper control. The power density obtained in the experi-
ments, to the best of our knowledge, is the highest reported
so far for supercapacitors using conducting polymer elec-
trodes, and is only surpassed by a supercapacitor based on
carbon nanotubes[2]Ðreported earlierÐwhich, however,
The electrodes were further characterized using impe-
dance measurements. The imaginary vs. the real compo-
nent of the total impedance at various frequencies for a
three-electrode electrochemical cell with polymer-coated
working electrodes is plotted in Figure 4. For the w-PED-
OT-PSS/PPy and the PEDOT-PSS/PPy electrodes, the high
frequency (low impedance) semicircular region, typical for
electrochemical systems, has a very small radius of curva-
ture, which indicates low resistance to the charge transfer
process.[4] In the former case the resistance is somewhat
smaller due to the higher water contentÐand hence more
open structureÐof the polymer. The resistance, however,
increases significantly if the electrode is once dried, giving
rise to an irreversible change in the material. The plot for
PEDOT-PSS-coated electrode shows a much larger radius
due to the lesser magnitude of the charge transfer (lower
capacitance) in the electrode. It is important to note the
frequencies corresponding to the region where the curves
in Figure 4 deviate from the semicircular region. This is in-
dicative of the ªfastnessº of the electrode. In all cases, apart
from the case of the dried electrodes, this frequency is
>75 Hz, which is rather large for any supercapacitor elec-
trode, comparable only with the value obtained with the
carbon nanotube electrode (100 Hz), mentioned earlier.[2]
For most supercapacitor electrodes, the value is not greater
It may be noted that the excellent performance of the
above supercapacitors should not eclipse the novelty of the
materials presented and their potential in various other ap-
plications. The main focus of the present work is the nov-
elty of the polymer electrode with its very high effective
surface area, and with its nanometer scale continuous con-
ducting network that extends into a large volume. The
large free volume available in the material may be used for
functionalization of the electrode with incorporation of
compounds with special properties that may find use in
electrocatalytic electrodes, sensors, electromembranes, etc.
The application to supercapacitors presented in this report
is only a demonstration of properties that are a conse-
quence of the unique structure of the material. The super-
capacitor performance may be optimized further by proper
choice of electrolyte, by use of more energy efficient redox
compounds,[17,18] substituting or complementing the PPy,
and by better design of the cell.[10,11]
For preparation of the PEDOT-PSS hydrogel electrode, 10 mg of the
polymer dispersion (Baytron-P, Bayer AG, Germany) was cast over a
cleaned 0.5 ´ 0.5 cm2 gold electrode at room temperature and then equili-
brated with aqueous 0.25 M MgSO4 for at least 2 h. The PEDOT-PSS/PPy
electrode was prepared by electropolymerizing pyrrole on a crosslinked
PEDOT-PSS electrode from an aqueous solution containing 0.25 M pyrrole
and 0.1 M NaSO4 at a pH of 2±3. A charge of 0.24 C was passed through the
cell at a constant potential of 0.5 V vs. an Ag/AgCl electrode. This results in
37.5 wt.-% PPy in the PEDOT-PSS/PPy electrode (molar ratio of PED-
OT:PPy monomer units was about 1:3), assuming 2.25 electrons per pyrrole
unit are needed for the polymerization of PPy at 25 % doping level. For the
preparation of the w-PEDOT-PSS/PPy, the dispersion on the gold electrode

was dried only to about 30 % of its weight before the crosslinking step. A

The PPy was grown on the polymer gel as described above. The unswelled
PEDOT-PSS/PPy electrode was prepared by electropolymerizing pyrrole on
an uncrosslinked PEDOT-PSS-coated electrode from acetonitrile solution
containing 0.25 M pyrrole and 0.1 M LiClO4 at a constant potential of
0.85 V vs. an Ag/AgCl electrode. The PPy-coated electrode was prepared
by growing PPy on a gold electrode under the same conditions as in the case
The electrochemical cell in the supercapacitor geometry for the cyclic
voltammetric and the chrono-potentiometric studies was a two-electrode
set-up, with two identical electrodes face to face and parallel, in aqueous
1 M Na2SO4. For the swelled-polymer electrodes, i.e., in the cases where
high power-density was applied the electrodes were at a fixed separation of
1 mm, whereas in other cases, the distance was about 1 cm. The cells were
in ambient atmospheric conditions. The chrono-potentiometric studies of
galvanostatic charging±discharging were carried out in the potential range
of 0 V to 0.8 V. For calculation of the energy and power densities, the first
discharge curve after equilibration of the cell at 0.8 V was analyzed, and the
dry mass of the polymeric material on a single electrode was considered.
The mass of the PEDOT-PSS was determined from the weight of the disper-
sion of known concentration that was applied to the electrode. The mass of
PPy was calculated from the coulomb of charge passed for its electropoly-
merization, assuming 2.25 electrons per pyrrole unit are needed for the poly-
merization of PPy at 25 % doping level.

By Thomas Bjùrnholm,* Tue Hassenkam,
Daniel R. Greve, Richard D. McCullough,
Manikandan Jayaraman, Steve M. Savoy, Chris E. Jones,
Devices based on conjugated polymers are becoming
commercially available in the form of light emitting
diodes,[1] proto-type electronic polymer circuits of conju-
gated polymers are presently being demonstrated to work[2]
and lasers are being developed.[3] All these applications
make use of the polymer samples as solution cast thin films.
Since the electronic and optical properties of the films
are inherently related to their local structure, it is vitally
important, for the performance of the devices, to achieve a
greater control of the self-assembly process that takes the
polymers from a disordered state in solution into a semi-
ordered solid state. The desire to make nanometer scale de-
vices for future electronic applications further emphasizes
the need for such nanoscale control.
Here we report on the formation of polythiophene nano-
wires. The work is a continuation of previously reported
studies[4] of Langmuir-Blodgett (LB) films of amphiphilic
polythiophenes which are substituted by alternating hydro-
philic and hydrophobic side groups along the backbone
(1 see Fig. 1A). Langmuir films of 1 form readily, and iso-
thermic compression (Fig. 1B) leads to densely packed
monolayers in which the polythiophene backbones are
p-stacked parallel to the water surface as indicated in
Figure 1A. The stacking of the polymers is highly ordered.
Adjacent polymers of 1 are displaced one thiophene unit
along the backbone, thereby allowing the alkyl chain of
one polymer to fill the void space between the alkyl chains
on the adjacent polymer.[4] From X-ray diffraction data,
the domain size of the highly ordered p-stacked polymer
has been estimated to be 6 nm in the stacking direction.[4]
The average length of the polymers is £ 40 nm (i.e. £ 50 re-
As described below, monolayers of 1 spontaneously fold
into wire-like structures when compressed beyond the col-
lapse point on the LB-trough. These wires are microns long
